title: Observations on the Economic Outlook, and Small Business
summary: Collins lays out a fairly benign 2026 baseline (solid growth, a relatively balanced labor market, and disinflation resuming later in the year as tariff effects fade) but with continued upside risks to inflation, which sat at 3% core PCE through end-2025 with no progress since 2024. She stresses returning inflation to target is essential and that resilient demand will keep some upward pressure on prices. On policy she favors a patient, deliberate approach, sees no urgency for additional adjustments, and would maintain rates at their current mildly restrictive level for some time, waiting for clear evidence inflation is moving durably toward 2 percent. Much of the speech addresses small-business cost pressures and New England conditions.

key_quotes.quote: I do not see an urgency for additional policy adjustments, and I will be looking for clear evidence that inflation is moving durably toward the 2 percent target – something that might occur only over the second half of the year.
key_quotes.context: Her near-term policy inclination: patient hold

key_quotes.quote: This, combined with recent evidence suggesting a relatively stable labor market, in my view argues for maintaining policy rates at their current, mildly restrictive levels for some time.
As a result, my outlook has demand exerting some upward pressure on prices, slowing the return of inflation to 2 percent.
key_quotes.context: Why she still sees upside inflation pressure

key_quotes.quote: Even with activity growing near capacity, and little inflationary pressures from the labor market, inflation has remained elevated.
key_quotes.context: Core PCE stuck at 3% with no progress since 2024
